FT_IC1.evidence: Prospective cohort data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 2, N=9799, U.S. ABCD cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis with linear and logistic regression models examining cyberbullying victimization associations with mental health and substance use outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper, Lancet Regional Health - Americas, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying victimization is the sole exposure variable; no separate phone/social media variable with its own coefficient reported. Per binding clarification: "Cyberbullying alone does not qualify. Cyberbullying counts only when paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient."

notes: Paper examines cyberbullying as exposure but lacks qualifying phone/SM variable; fails FT-IC2 mechanical Step 1 and Step 2

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 3 (2019-2021), 10,305 U.S. early adolescents

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using multiple regression analyses examining associations between bedtime screen use and sleep outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published paper, Sleep Health: Journal of the National Sleep Foundation, 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Bedtime screen use composite includes multi-device items without per-device breakout: "watching movies, videos, or TV shows; playing video games; playing music; talking on the phone or texting; spending time online on social media; browsing the Internet; and using a computer/laptop for studying." Per binding rule: "Multi-device aggregated time fails Step 2... NOT_MET unless a per-device breakout (cellphone alone) exists somewhere." No phone/SM-only variable with separate coefficient reported in supplementary or sensitivity analyses.

notes: Composite screen use variable bundles TV, gaming (platform unspecified), music, non-phone computer use without isolation; fails FT-IC2 Step 2

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Year 3 (2019-2022), 10,048 U.S. early adolescents

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using multiple linear regression examining associations between media parenting practices and adolescent screen use and problematic use outcomes

FT_IC2.evidence: Exposures are parenting practices (parent screen modeling, mealtime screen use, bedroom screen use, parental monitoring, limiting); outcomes are SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), and MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire). While SMAQ/MPIQ/VGAQ are SM/phone variables, the other side of the relationship is parenting practices (parent-level contextual/behavioral variables), not adolescent individual-trait-level outcomes. Per §2.4: "even if SM/phone variable qualifies, the OTHER side must be individual-trait-level... on the other side → NOT_MET."

notes: Paper examines parenting practices as predictors of SM/phone problematic use; other side is contextual (parenting behavior) rather than adolescent individual-trait outcome; fails FT-IC2
